sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient presents with a 2-3 week history of a non-healing wound on the right foot, which initially started as a blister due to neuropathy caused by wearing a new pair of shoes.
The patient had previously seen a podiatrist but was unable to continue due to their relocation.
The patient denies any trauma to the foot and reports no pain or discomfort in the wound area.
The patient also mentions that the wound sticks to the sock when removed, but there is no drainage, fever, or chills.
The patient has a history of diabetes, with an A1c of slightly over 8.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of diabetes and has previously seen a podiatrist for foot issues.
Review of systems: The patient denies any other symptoms such as fever, chills, or drainage from the wound.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's vital signs are as follows: temperature 98.1°F, heart rate 72, respirations 16, blood pressure 110/65.

Physical exam: The physical exam reveals a 1x2 inch circular wound on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, proximal to the right fifth MTP joint, with yellow slough and minimal granulation tissue.
There is no surrounding erythema or cellulitis, no fluid collection, no necrosis, and no odor.
The patient has palpable bilateral femoral and popliteal pulses, but no palpable DP or PT pulses with Doppler signs present.
Test Results: The patient's right foot X-ray shows no evidence of osteomyelitis.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a diabetic foot ulcer with compromised wound healing due to neuropathy caused by poorly fitting shoes.
Problem list: Diabetic foot ulcer; Compromised wound healing; Diabetes mellitus; Neuropathy.
Plan:
I prescribed collagenase ointment to be applied to the wound once daily, covered with a dry sterile dressing.
I will perform a debridement in the office to remove dead tissue.
I will order an Ankle Brachial Index (ABI) to determine the blood supply to the foot.
I want the patient to wear a surgical shoe to take pressure off the area.
I will see the patient again in two weeks to reassess the wound.
I may refer the patient to a vascular specialist if the ABI indicates poor blood supply for wound healing.
I will also refer the patient to a diabetic specialist to discuss ways to manage their diabetes and reduce their A1c.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen again in two weeks.
